coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) detected to mercury ions in food monitoring
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shan, Di
Yu, Huichuan
Yang, Zhichao
Li, Hongda
Jia, Rulin
Zhang, Yue
coredata.dc:description: Using 2,3-diaminopyridine and citric acid as precursors, blue fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with a narrow size distribution (∼7.2 nm) were prepared and applied in the following assay for mercury ion detection at a weight ratio of 2,3-diaminopyridine:citric acid = 1:1 (0.2 g: 0.2 g, 20 mL for H2O), 220 °C, and 10 h. NCQDs was characterized by TEM, FT-IR, XPS, UV–Vis and EDS, and the prepared NCQDs display excitation-independent behavior due to less surface defects and uniform size. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the NCQDs were 380 nm and 430 nm, respectively. Interestingly, the fluorescence of the NCQDs could be rapidly and selectively quenched by Hg2+ within 9 min at room temperature without further modification. Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection (LOD) was measured to be at the nanomolar level (42.4 nmol/L) with a linear range of 0–5.0 μmol/L, and fluorescence analysis of NCQDs was successfully used for the qualitative and quantitative analysis of mercury ions in food samples. Furthermore, our results revealed that fluorescence quenching occurred under the common fluences of the inner filter effect, and the static quenching effect was authenticated in the process in which Hg2+ coordinates with the NCQDs to form nonfluorescent complexes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Blue fluorescent
Detection of Hg2+
Real sample

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCQDs)
pH effects on the optical characteristics of NCQDs and NCQDs/Hg2+
The response time, selectivity, and anti-interference of NCQDs for Hg2+
The fluorescence response of NCQDs for Hg2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Influence of reaction temperature and ratio of 2,3-diaminopyridine and citric acid on the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCQDs with a ratio of 2,3-diaminopyridine and citric acid = 1:1 at 220 °C for different reaction time. (c) The storing time of NCQDs. (d) The influence of ionic strengths on the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs.
(a) TEM Image of NCQDs; (b) FTIR Spectrum of NCQDs; (c) XPS Spectrum of NCQDs; High-Resolution XPS Spectrum of NCQDs: (d) C 1 s, (e) N 1 s, (f) O 1 s.
The dispersion of the main elements (a), C elements (b), N elements (c), and O elements (d) for NCQDs.

(a) 3D-fluorescence topographical map of NCQDs; (b) The UV–vis absorption spectrum, excitation spectrum, and emission spectrum of NCQDs; (b) Inset: image of NCQDs aqueous solution under visible light (left) and ultraviolet light (right).

(a) pH effect of NCQDs and NCQDs for Hg2+. (b) Fluorescence intensities (λex/λem = 380/430 nm) of NCQDs in the presence or non-presence for various analytes (1 for only NCQDs, 2–15 for Hg2+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Ag+, NO3 −, SO4 2−, Cl−.)
(a) Fluorescence spectra of NCQDs with different concentrations of Hg2+ (λex/λem = 380/430 nm, 0–50 μmol/L,) (b) Linear correlation plot of Hg2+ centration in the Range of 0–5 μmol/L.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Hg2+ (black), and fluorescence excitation spectrum of NCQDs (red). (b) Fluorescence intensity decay traces of NCQDs before (purple curve) and after (blue curve) addition of Hg2+. (c)) The Stern-Volmer graphs of NCQDs for Hg2+. (d) FT-IR spectra of NCQDs without (black line) and with Hg2+ (red). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Preparation and sensing mechanism of NCQDs
Results of Hg 2+ sensing in real samples with NCQDs.
Comparison of this method with a traditional methods for Hg2+ from grass shrimp and rice samples.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) detected to mercury ions in food monitoring

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Forensic Science and Technology, Criminal Investigation Police University of China, Liaoning, Shenyang 110035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Forensic Science and Technology
Criminal Investigation Police University of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Forensic Science and Technology, Criminal Investigation Police University of China, Liaoning Shenyang, 110035, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using 2,3-diaminopyridine and citric acid as precursors, blue fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with a narrow size distribution (∼7.2 nm) were prepared and applied in the following assay for mercury ion detection at a weight ratio of 2,3-diaminopyridine:citric acid = 1:1 (0.2 g: 0.2 g, 20 mL for H2O), 220 °C, and 10 h. NCQDs was characterized by TEM, FT-IR, XPS, UV–Vis and EDS, and the prepared NCQDs display excitation-independent behavior due to less surface defects and uniform size. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the NCQDs were 380 nm and 430 nm, respectively. Interestingly, the fluorescence of the NCQDs could be rapidly and selectively quenched by Hg2+ within 9 min at room temperature without further modification. Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection (LOD) was measured to be at the nanomolar level (42.4 nmol/L) with a linear range of 0–5.0 μmol/L, and fluorescence analysis of NCQDs was successfully used for the qualitative and quantitative analysis of mercury ions in food samples. Furthermore, our results revealed that fluorescence quenching occurred under the common fluences of the inner filter effect, and the static quenching effect was authenticated in the process in which Hg2+ coordinates with the NCQDs to form nonfluorescent complexes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution has attracted much attention because it can accumulate along the food chain and pose a great threat to human health and environmental protection. (Kitte et al., 2019) Heavy metals are non-biodegradable elements, and heavy metal ions have strong affinity for biological tissues, which makes them difficult to eliminate in human body. The intake of metal ions will cause great damage. Industries related to mining, textile and petrochemical industries have increased the use of compounds and substances based on metal ions, resulting in the pollution of drinking water sources and food. (Rebolledo-Perales et al., 2021)

Mercury ion (Hg2+) is a highly toxic heavy metal ion that is easily soluble in water, once Hg2+ entering human body through respiratory tract or skin will cause irreversible damage to human brain, vision and nervous system. (Sivakumar & Lee, 2021) There are also many reports on mercury ion pollution in food. (Xia et al., 2024)Currently, for Hg2+ detection methods mainly include atomic absorption/emission spectrometry (Shirkhanloo et al., 2015), X-ray absorption spectrometry (George et al., 2010), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (Tercanli et al., 2024) and ion chromatography (Thirumalai et al., 2018) etc., the practical application of these methods is greatly limited by its instrument price and equipment operation difficulty. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple, low-cost, easy-to-prepare and low-detection method for Hg2+.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a special class of the quantum dots family, have showcased outstanding performance in various scientific fields since their initial discovery in 2004. Presently, these nanoscaled moieties find widespread applications in photocatalysis (Tian et al., 2021; Xu, Li, et al., 2023), photodegradation (Mei et al., 2022; Wang, Chen, et al., 2023), optoelectronics (Wang et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2017), bioimaging (Karakoçak et al., 2021; Yue, Li, Liu, et al., 2019), and biomedicine as antibacterial agents (Wang et al., 2022; Wen et al., 2023) and drug carriers (Karami et al., 2023; Su et al., 2020). Additionally, the unique optical properties of CQDs have positioned them as fluorescence probes for various substances, including metal cations (Gurung et al., 2023; Silva et al., 2024), inorganic anions (Boruah et al., 2020; Yue et al., 2021), and organic molecules (Guan et al., 2017; Hu & Gao, 2020; Wu & Yan, 2017).The principle behind detecting metal ions using carbon dots primarily involves supramolecular interactions (i.e., coordination). The abundant electron-rich groups (such as amino, hydroxyl, sulfhydryl) on the surface of carbon dots coordinate with various metal ions, leading to non-radiative electron transfer (Yang et al., 2015). As a result, the fluorescence yield is significantly lowered, often manifested as fluorescence quenching (Kai et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2022; Yue, Li, Cao, et al., 2019).

At present, there are some reports about carbon dots used for the detection of mercury ions. Wang et al. (Wang, Huang, et al., 2023) prepared carbon dots/copper nanoclusters double fluorescence emission complex (CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs) based on silica coating, and detected Hg2+ in water by the change of fluorescence color under ultraviolet irradiation, but the detection limit was high (1 μM). Rasul (Barvin et al., 2019) et al. used carbon point modified electrode prepared from carbonized coconut shell to detect Hg2+, and achieved a lower detection limit (2.5 nmol/L). However, this method is more complicated than fluorescence detection, and it is difficult to achieve rapid detection in the field. Ren (Ren et al., 2018) and others developed blue fluorescent carbon dots synthesized by urea and sodium citrate in DMF for cell imaging and mercury ion detection, but they did not try to apply them to actual sample analysis.

Therefore, in view of the harmfulness of mercury ions in food and the current imperfect research situation, it is necessary to develop new carbon dots for detecting mercury ions. We developed a nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCQDs) with rich amino and carboxyl groups on its surface for the detection of Hg2+, the NCQDs has high fluorescence quantum yield and good selectivity for Hg2+, and can detect mercury ions by fluorescence quenching. The NCQDs has been successfully applied to the detection of mercury ions in tap water and rice. This study will be helpful for the rapid detection of mercury ions in food.

2,3-Diaminopyridine，citric acid, ethanol,PbCl2, Zn(NO3)2•6H2O, NiCl3•6H2O, KCl, NaCl, FeCl3•6H2O, MgCl2, CuSO4•5H2O, AgNO3, CaCl2, MgCl2•6H2O, CdCl2, were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Grass shrimp, Chinese Mitten Crab, Ctenopharyngodon idella and rice were purchased from Walmart (Shenyang). Ultrapure water was is prepared by the laboratory. Tap water is taken from the laboratory.
Unless otherwise specified, all reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare NCQDs, 0.2 g of 2, 3-diaminopyridine and 0.2 g citric acid was dissolved in 20 mL of water and sonicated for 20 min after stirring with a glass rod. The solution was then transferred to a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 10 h in an oven. After cooling, the product mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered through a nylon membrane (0.44 μm). The filtrate was contained in a 3500 Da dialysis bag and immersed in ultrapure water for 12 h. Subsequently, the reddish-brown solution inside the bag was freeze-dried, yielding 285 mg of brown carbon dot powder (NCQDs).

Fluorescence measurements were conducted using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (G9800A, Agilent Technologies, USA) with a 10 mm quartz cuvette. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU Corporation, Japan) with a 1 cm quartz cell. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed using a Nicolet iS50 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) in transmission mode with KBr pellets. The morphology and size of the prepared carbon quantum dots were determined using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Hitachi-600, Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a PHI 5702 spectrometer (Thermo scientific K-Alpha, America). The imaging of transmission electron microscope was completed on JEM-2100 (JEOL Ltd., Japan).

A stock solution of NCQDs (500 mg/L) was prepared by dispersing 50 mg of the powder in 50 mL of water through sonication and diluting it to 100 mL. This solution was stored at 5 °C in a refrigerator for further use.

For selectivity evaluation, a NCQDs (20 mg/L), Hg2+solution (5 μmol/L) and an interference (50 μmol/L) [PbCl2, Zn(NO3)2•6H2O, NiCl3•6H2O, KCl, NaCl, FeCl3•6H2O, MgCl2, CuSO4•5H2O, AgNO3, CaCl2, MgCl2•6H2O, CdCl2 in water] were mixed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) buffer solution at pH 7.0. The solution was allowed to stand for 25 min before fluorescence measurements.

For fluorescence response, a NCQDs solution (20 mg/L) and various Hg2+ concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 8.0, 10.0, 25.0, 50.0 μmol/L) were mixed with PBS buffer solution at pH 7.0. The solutions were allowed to stand for 25 min before fluorescence measurements.
All the fluorescence measurements were conducted at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm.

Highly photoluminescent NCQDs were successfully fabricated through a facile hydrothermal route by carbonizing a mixture of 2,3-diaminopyridine and citric acid. Previous research has shown that the fluorescence behavior of carbon quantum dots is influenced by a variety of factors, including the surface state, surface passivation, and carbonaceous core. To create highly luminescent and water-soluble carbon quantum dots, the reaction conditions were optimized by adjusting the ratio of 2,3-diaminopyridine to citric acid, the reaction temperature, and the reaction time.

The influence of the reaction temperature and 2, 3-diaminopyridine: citric acid ratio (by weight) on the fluorescence properties of the product is shown in Fig. 1 . Only when the ratio of 2,3-diaminopyridine to citric acid (weight ratio) was set at 1:1 and the reaction temperature was set at 220 °C, intense fluorescence was observed for the as-prepared NCQDs (Fig. 1a). The dramatic increase in the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs may stem from nitrogen doping of the carbon core (Li et al., 2021; Li, Zhang, et al., 2020). Subsequently, the effect of the reaction time on the carbonation process was also investigated (fixed 2,3-diaminopyridine:citric acid = 1:1, weight ratio). The highest fluorescence intensity was achieved when the carbonization time was 10 h (Fig. 1b). When the reaction time was further increased to 12 h, the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly. Upon further increasing the reaction time to 14 h, the fluorescence intensity decreased, which may be attributed to the aggregation of NCQDs due to the longer carbonation time (Li, Zhang, et al., 2020). In addition, the reaction time did not affect the fluorescence emission wavelength of the NCQDs. Based on the above preliminary experimental results, highly luminescent NCQDs were prepared and applied in the following assay for Hg2+ detection at a molar ratio of 2,3-diaminopyridine:citric acid = 1:1 (weight ratio, 0.2 g: 0.2 g, 20 mL for H2O), 220 °C, and 10 h. (See Scheme 1.)

Considering that the fluorescent stability of probes is a key concern for sensing behavior and practical applications of fluorescent sensors, the fluorescence emission of NCQDs was further estimated. The NCQDs exhibited a small attenuation in fluorescence intensity after storage for 60 day at 4 °C (Fig. 1c), signifying their favorable optical stability. These phenomena may be due to the surface functional groups and the stable chemical structure of the NCQDs. To study the stability of NCQDs under high ionic strength conditions, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs at 430 nm was recorded in solutions containing different concentrations of NaCl (aq). As illustrated in Fig. 1d, when the concentration of NaCl (aq) varies from 0.2 to 2.0 mol/L, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs remains almost constant, indicating the great resistance of NCQDs to high ionic strength environments. The fluorescence stability of NCQDs under high-salt conditions ensures their successful application in sensing Hg2+ under extreme environmental conditions.

The NCQDs dispersed uniformly in water. Fig. 2a shows the NCQDs under TEM. These presented a spherical morphology, with particle sizes ranging from 5.2 to 9.5 nm, averaging at ∼7.2 nm (Fig. 2a inset). High-resolution TEM images (Fig. 2a inset) revealed lattice fringes of graphitic carbon (sp2) with a spacing of ∼0.23 nm, indicating the crystalline nature of the prepared NCQDs (Yang et al., 2018).

FTIR spectra of the NCQDs exhibited peaks at 3408 cm−1 and 3228 cm−1, attributed to -OH and -NH bending vibrations, while the peak at 1712 cm−1 and 1625 corresponds to CC and CO stretching vibrations (Xu, Zhang, et al., 2023). The additional peaks at 1395 cm−1 attributed to CN stretching vibrations (Fig. 2b) (Gao et al., 2023). The peak at 1130 cm−1 represents CO (Safieh et al., 2020).XPS revealed the elemental composition of the NCQDs, with peaks at 284, 399, and 530 eV representing C1s, N1s, and O1s, as shown in Fig. 2c. The relative intensities indicated that the NCQDs contained carbon (67.62 %), nitrogen (15.88 %), and oxygen (16.5 %) according to the number of atoms. Furthermore, the resolved spectra (Fig. 2d∼f) of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s showed peaks at 284.7, 285.4, and 286.5 eV, attributed to C=C/C–C, CN and CO bonds, respectively (Naik et al., 2020), the peak of 288.4 eV represents the presence of carboxyl groups in the NCQDs. (Jiao et al., 2020)On the other hand, the N 1 s spectrum comprised of four sub-peaks representing pyridinic N (398.6 eV), amino N (399.1 eV), pyrrolic N (399.9 eV), and graphitic N (400.8 eV) (Dong et al., 2022). Moreover, oxygen existed in CO and CO forms, as evidenced by the peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.3 eV, respectively (Naik et al., 2020). Fig. 3 displays the elemental mapping of pure NCQDs using a scanning electron micrograph (SEM) and an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum. The EDS mapping spectra confirmed the chemical composition of the NCQDs, revealing the main elements to be carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O). This confirmed the presence and uniform distribution of nitrogen within the NCQDs.

The results indicated that under hydrothermal conditions, 2,3-diaminopyridine and citric acid followed complex polymerization, intermolecular cyclization, and condensation reactions (Ding et al., 2016). These also revealed the presence of amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the NCQDs, which can participate in coordination reactions (Vedamalai et al., 2014).

The 3D-fluorescence, UV–vis absorption, Excitation, and Emission spectra of NCQDs and NCQDs/Hg2+ were recorded and shown in Fig. 4 . With pure water as the solvent and the excitation wavelength ranging from 300 to 425 nm, the NCQDs displayed a predominant emission peak around 430 nm independent of the excitation wavelength (Fig. 4a). Upon the excitation at a wavelength of 380 nm, the NCQDs demonstrated a photoluminescent response with an emission spectrum peaks at approximately 430 nm. This emission corresponds to the visible light range associated with blue coloration. Furthermore, when subjected to 365 nm ultraviolet light, the NCQDs displayed an intense and vivid blue fluorescence, a phenomenon that was visually confirmed in the inset of Fig. 4b. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the NCQDs (Fig. 4b) displayed a characteristic absorption peak at 283 nm, which correspond to n–π* transitions of the C=O/C=N bonds. (Zhao et al., 2022) Furthermore, the Quantum yield (QY) of the NCQDs was calculated to be ∼31.7 % (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH effects on the optical characteristics of NCQDs and NCQDs/Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of NCQDs and NCQDs / Hg2+ measured at different pH values ranging from 2 to 12 are shown in Fig. 5a. It indicated that the fluorescence intensity of the NCQDs at 430 nm (Fig. 5a blue point) varied with the pH and showed the highest intensity at a pH of 7. This observation suggests that the variation in pH possibly influenced the functional groups on the surface of the NCQDs, altering the electron energies (Liu et al., 2021). For the NCQDs/Hg2+ system, Fig. 5a (green point) shows the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs at different pH in the system containing 5 μmol/L Hg2+. Compared with NCQDs, it has the highest quenching efficiency at pH 7, which may be that NCQDs has the strongest binding effect with Hg2+ in neutral environment, so the quenching degree is the highest. Consequently, the PBS buffer solution of pH 7.0 (10 mmol/L) was selected for the test experiments of the samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response time, selectivity, and anti-interference of NCQDs for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The response time, selectivity, and anti-interference capabilities yielded satisfactory results, as shown in Figs. S1 ∼ S3 and 5b. For instance, NCQDs showed response times of ∼15, ∼ 12.5 and ∼ 9 min for 1.0, 2.5, and 5.0 μmol/L Hg2+, respectively. In the subsequent section, a duration of 20 min was allocated for the testing of both simulated and real samples (Figs. S1). The selectivity and anti-interference capability of the NCQDs (20 mg/L) for Hg2+ (5 μmol/L) were evaluated in the presence of 50 μmol/L of various possible interferences (including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Ag+, NO3 −, SO4 2−, Cl−). Compared to the various other metal ions, Hg2+ was found to exhibit apparently lower fluorescence signal when reacted with the NCQDs. Fig. 5b shows that the Ag+ had a relative fluorescence intensity of 0.94, while the Hg2+ had a lower one of 0.46. These observations enable the easy identification of the Hg2+ from other cations. In the presence of the anti-interference, the Hg2+ resulted in a similar response exhibiting almost no interference in the presence of other competing analytes (Fig. 5b). The results suggested that NCQDs had high selectivity and anti-interference ability with a great promise for detecting Hg2+ under complex conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence response of NCQDs for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitative fluorescence response of NCQDs (20 mg/L) for different concentrations of Hg2+ (0–50 μmol/L) is shown in Fig. 6a. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases with the addition of Hg2+, exhibiting a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs solution and Hg2+ concentration (0–5 μmol/L). As shown in Fig. 6b, this relationship was evaluated as 1-FI/FI0 = 0.132[C] + 0.002 (R2 = 0.9909), where FI/FI0 represents the ratio of the fluorescence intensity after adding Hg2+ to the initial fluorescence intensity, and [C] denotes the concentration of Hg2+ in the system. Employing the 3σ rule (Shortreed et al., 1996), the detection limit of NCQDs was calculated to be ∼42.4 nmol/L Hg2+, demonstrating a high level of sensitivity. A comparison of our proposed method with recently reported Hg2+-sensing strategies is summarized in Table S2, which shown that NCQDs was superior to some reported fluorescent probes, demonstrating the feasibility of detecting Hg2+ in NCQDs.

By analyzing their absorption spectrum and fluorescence decay, NCQDs' detection mechanism for Hg2+ should be examined in order to clarify the functional performance. The excitation spectrum of NCQDs and the absorption spectrum of Hg2+ were shown in Fig. 7a. The Hg2+ appeared to exhibit a strong absorption band at 300 nm, which overlapped with the excitation band of NCQDs at 380 nm. This observation may rule out the charge transfer quenching process (Li, Zhao, et al., 2020). Thus, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or the inner filter effect (IFE) could be the cause of the Hg2+-induced fluorescence quenching (Li et al., 2021; Zhang, Lv, et al., 2021). Further measurements of the fluorescence lifetime were required in order to gain a better understanding of the underlying quenching mechanism. The fluorescence decay profiles of NCQDs and NCQDs+Hg2+ with almost unchanged with fluorescence lifetimes of 6.42 and 6.61 ns, respectively (Fig. 7b). These results indicated that the quenching mechanism belonged to the IFE mechanism instead of the FRET mechanism because no significant shortening of the fluorescence lifetime was observed (Zhang, Tan, & Hu, 2021). IFE generally refers to the fact that the excitation or emission spectra of the fluorophores were absorbed by the quencher to reduce or even quench the fluorescence intensity (Zhang, Lv, et al., 2021), which indicated that the absorption position of Hg2+ had the potential to easily eliminate the excitation of the probe, causing quenching of the fluorescence of NCQDs (Fig. 7a).

Because fluorescence quenching is generally determined by the coexistence of several parameters (Li et al., 2021), the possibility of the dynamic quenching effect (DQE) and Static Quenching Effect (SQE) mechanisms was also discussed in this work. The fluorescence quenching was analyzed via the Stern-Volmer Eq. (Tang et al., 2020) as follows:
(1)
FI 0
/ FI = 1 + Ksv
Q
= 1 + Kq • τ0
Q
where FI and FI0 represent the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs with and without Hg2+, respectively; [Q] represents the concentration of Hg2+, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, Kq denotes the quenching rate constant, and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime (6.42 ns). Based on the linear equation (FI0/FI = 0.31× + 0.98, R2 = 0.9986), the Stern-Volmer (Fig. 7c) constant Ksv corresponded to 3.1 × 105 L • mol−1. According to Ksv = Kq•τ0, Kq was calculated to be 4.83 × 1013 L • mol−1 • s−1, which is higher than the maximum diffusion-controlled limit (2.0 × 1010 L • mol−1 • s−1), demonstrating SQE mechanism of the process (Tang et al., 2020). According to the literature, the formation of a non-fluorescent complex occurs between the fluorophore and the quenching agent in the SQE process (Li, Zhang, et al., 2020). Thus, the fluorescence quenching of NCQDs may be caused by the chelating reaction of Hg2+with the functional groups on the surface of the NCQDs (Xu et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 7d, the FT-IR spectra of NCQDs with and without Hg2+ were compared. After reaction with Hg2+, the CN bending modes were strengthened and significantly blue-shifted to 1395 cm−1. the CO band at 1130 cm−1 was blue-shifted to 1109 cm−1. Notably, the carbonyl group at 1625 cm−1 not only showed an evident blue shift to 1580 cm−1 but also weakened the intensity of the stretching vibration absorption band. The above results indicate that Hg2+ chelated with NCQDs via CO, -OH, CN, NH, and CO groups. Therefore, fluorescence quenching occurred under the common fluences of IFE and SQE.

In a word, in this work, the detection of Hg2+ was realized by using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCQDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield synthesized by hydrothermal method. It was applied to the detection of actual samples. In the buffer system with pH 7, the aqueous solution of carbon dots has high fluorescence quantum yield and good selectivity for mercury ions. There is a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of aqueous solution of NCQDs and the concentration of mercury ions (1-F/F0 = 0.132[C] + 0.002, R2 = 0.9909) and a low detection limit (42.4 nmol/L). Good results have also been obtained in the detection of actual samples (grass shrimp, Chinese Mitten Crab, Ctenopharyngodon idella, and rice), and the recovery rate is between 96.0 % and 103.0 %. The precision of NCQDs was affirmed through a side-by-side comparison with atomic absorption spectroscopy (GB 5009.17–2021), yielding concordant results that vouch for the method's dependability. Additionally, it was demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of NCQDs for Hg2+ was due to the combined effects of IFE and SQE.

Di Shan: Writing – review & editing. Huichuan Yu: Writing – original draft. Zhichao Yang: Writing – review & editing. Hongda Li: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Rulin Jia: Validation. Yue Zhang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

Hongda Li reports was provided by Criminal Investigation Police University of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804140), Science and Technology of the Ministry of Public Security of the People's Republic of China (No. 2022JC02), the Shenyang Young and Middle-aged Science and Technology Innovation Talent Support Program (No. RC220480), Project of Applied Basic Research Program from Liaoning Province (No. 2023JF12/101300097).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.141308.
